title: The accounting cycle: journal → ledger → trial balance

questions.explanation: The front half of the cycle runs analyze → journalize → post → unadjusted trial balance. Posting transfers each journal line into its general-ledger account; the trial balance comes only after posting.
The general journal is the book of original entry: every transaction is first recorded there, chronologically and complete in one place, before being posted to the ledger.

Supplies (an asset) increases, so it is debited. The purchase is on account, so the claim created is Accounts Payable (a liability), which increases with a credit. No cash moved, and no expense is recognized until the supplies are used.

Equal debits and credits were posted — just to the wrong asset account. The trial balance's column equality is only an arithmetic parity check; it cannot detect wrong-account postings, omitted entries, or duplicated entries.

The difference is 87,840 − 87,300 = 540, and 540 ÷ 9 = 60 — a whole number, the classic fingerprint of a transposition or slide. An entry omitted or posted twice in full keeps the trial balance balanced, so neither explains any difference.

Debit-normal accounts: Cash 9,800 + Accounts Receivable 4,300 + Supplies 1,200 + Equipment 15,000 + Rent Expense 2,500 + Salaries Expense 2,200 = 35,000. (The credit column — AP 3,600 + Notes Payable 8,000 + Common Stock 12,000 + Service Revenue 11,400 — also totals 35,000.)
A debit posted as a credit understates the debit column and overstates the credit column, so the totals diverge by twice the amount: difference = 53,000 − 52,140 = 860; 860 ÷ 2 = 430.
